translation.content: What does the future feel like in 2026? We live in a nostalgic era, and across electronic music, revivalism is well and truly back. Even techno—a genre inspired by the promise of tomorrow—has begun to feel strangely stale. Enter Gautham Garg, AKA Decoder. Raised in Dallas, the 21-year-old is delivering a compelling vision of futurism for our current moment. Long before he could legally enter a club, Garg was producing. He first opened Fruity Loops aged 11, inspired by video game soundtracks and his mother's synth pop collection. By 17, he'd released his debut album, Dark Shape, on Jeff Mills' Axis Records, envisioning a world shaped by digital authoritarianism and intergalactic conflict. It revealed an unusual sense of depth: tripped-out tones and restrained atmospheres offset by moments of lightness and play, and that wide-eyed perspective remains audible in his work today. Parallels have been drawn with techno's canonical futurists, most notably Mills and Richie Hawtin, but listen closely to RA.1023 and a broader palette emerges. Twinkling, microtonal flourishes recall the Colundi-era work of Aleksi Perälä, while the patient frameworks hint more towards Perlon-era minimalism than the severe slammers of the early 2000s (note the Ricardo Villalobos and Margaret Dygas nods in the tracklist). On RA.1023, Garg presents his take on techno for this decade. Like many of his sets, it's built from unreleased tracks, either from his labels or his own productions. Yes, there's heft, but it's more about body than bite: the drums are elastic and finely tuned, and even when the atmosphere gets choppier, a buoyant hypnotism remains. Garg's music is often described as sci-fi, but where this can often skew cold or austere, optimism shines through here. In Garg's hands, the future of techno still sounds bright. - Bella Aquilina What have you been up to recently? I've been touring a lot lately and working on some exciting projects for my labels Codec and Toca, as well as preparing for my upcoming album release on Planet X. Also getting back into fingerboarding in my spare time. How and where was the mix recorded? And can you tell us the idea behind it? This mix was recorded at home in Berlin, where I've recently moved. The past few months of my life have been pretty chaotic, as I went on my first Asia tour, debuted at Berghain and speed ran four shows in India, among other life things in between. I wanted to use this mix as a way to emotionally kind of process it all, and I hope you can hear all the different peaks and valleys throughout the journey! I did it all in one take, and wanted it to be a little rough around the edges, reflecting a more honest version of how I like to mix at home. Not super polished, but just having fun with the tracks and seeing how they play together. Most of the tracks are unreleased from friends or my latest studio sessions, as well as some stuff that's going to be out on my label Codec soon. What are you looking forward to in the near future? I'm very much looking forward to the release of my album Prakasa, on Exos's label Planet X. It's been over three years in the making, and it represents all the different sides to my sound in one cohesive project. We've also made a music video for one of the tracks, and there's a lot of other cool things we have planned which I'm super excited to share with the world. Aside from that, my label partner Jay York and I have been scheming a lot for our future Toca releases. My other label Codec turns five this year, and I have a special project in the works for that too! What's one social or political cause you want the world to pay more attention to? I hope more artists stop paying attention to all the noise around them and start making art that's authentic to them. Institutions and the scene as a whole places way too much importance on public opinion, and it's really detrimental to artists who are trying to develop their own voice. I hope people take more risks with their art so we don't have an oversaturation of copy-paste music, so that people who engage with that art are also challenged to see things outside of the status quo. What's one club or party that had a major impact on you as an artist? I can name three! Body Mechanics, Voidware and Freqsystem were three parties I attended early on in my music journey when I was still in high school back in Texas, and seeing that DIY attitude early on was super inspiring to me during a time when I felt very isolated from the music world. I have great memories from those parties and I learned a lot from them! It gave me a sense of hope to keep going and offered me a community during a time when I felt pretty disconnected from a lot of people around me.
translation.blurb: The Texan prodigy transmits the sound of sci-fi techno in 2026.
